_note: Evergreen soccer education content for non-live placements. Rule cards cite IFAB. Tactics cards are editorial explainers, not official rule text.

audiences.label: First Match
audiences.goal: Help a first-time viewer follow a game in under a minute.

audiences.label: Watching Smart
audiences.goal: Give casual fans a few sharp things to notice during a match.

audiences.label: Soccer Brain
audiences.goal: Offer deeper tactical language without turning the site into a coaching clinic.

cards.title: The team with more goals wins
cards.hook: Soccer is low scoring, so one clean finish can flip everything.
cards.summary: A goal counts when the whole ball crosses the whole goal line between the posts and under the crossbar.
cards.whyItMatters: A match can feel even for long stretches, then change because one attack is sharper than the other.
cards.whatToWatch: Notice how much emotion follows a single chance on target.

cards.title: The clock lies a little
cards.hook: Ninety minutes does not mean exactly 90:00 of playing time.
cards.summary: Matches have two 45-minute halves, but referees add time for stoppages like goals, substitutions, injuries, time wasting, and VAR checks.
cards.whyItMatters: Late goals are common because the end of each half usually stretches longer than the scoreboard clock suggests.
cards.whatToWatch: When the board shows added time, the game usually gets more frantic, not less.

cards.title: Offside is about timing, not camping
cards.hook: A player is not punished just for standing high up the field.
cards.summary: Offside is judged when a team-mate plays the ball. A player is only punished if they then get involved in play or interfere with an opponent.
cards.whyItMatters: This is why TV replays freeze the instant the pass is made, not the instant the runner touches the ball.
cards.whatToWatch: Watch the passer's foot, then the runner's line against the second-last defender.

cards.title: Not every foul is a card
cards.hook: A whistle and a booking are not the same thing.
cards.summary: Careless fouls can be punished without a card. Reckless challenges usually mean yellow. Excessive force usually means red.
cards.whyItMatters: Fans often think every hard challenge should be a booking, but the Laws separate bad timing from dangerous play.
cards.whatToWatch: The referee's body language often tells you whether they saw a simple foul or misconduct.

cards.title: A penalty is not a guaranteed goal
cards.hook: It is a huge chance, but it is still a duel.
cards.summary: A penalty kick is taken from the spot with the ball stationary, the kicker clearly identified, and the goalkeeper on or behind the goal line until the kick.
cards.whyItMatters: The moment feels automatic, but pressure, technique, and goalkeeper timing still matter a lot.
cards.whatToWatch: Watch whether the kicker is reading the goalkeeper or simply choosing power and placement.

cards.title: Draws are real, and restarts matter
cards.hook: Soccer does not force a winner every time.
cards.summary: A group-stage match can end level. Play restarts with kick-offs, free kicks, throw-ins, goal kicks, corner kicks, penalties, or dropped balls depending on what happened.
cards.whyItMatters: A draw can be useful in tournament play, and many big chances start from well-used restarts.
cards.whatToWatch: Late in group games, pay attention to whether both teams are still chasing the win or protecting the table.

cards.title: More possession does not automatically mean better
cards.hook: Some teams want the ball. Others want the moment after they win it.
cards.summary: A favorite may dominate possession while an underdog waits for transition chances. The prettier team is not always the more dangerous team.
cards.whyItMatters: This helps new viewers understand why a team with less of the ball can still look comfortable.
cards.whatToWatch: Ask who is creating the cleaner shots, not just who is passing more.

cards.title: The game changes fastest right after the ball changes hands
cards.hook: The most dangerous moment can be the first three seconds after a turnover.
cards.summary: Transitions are the instant a team switches from attack to defense or defense to attack.
cards.whyItMatters: World Cup matches often swing on chaos before defenses reset.
cards.whatToWatch: When a move breaks down, see which side reacts first instead of which side complains first.

cards.title: Wide players stretch the field so others can breathe
cards.hook: A winger hugging the touchline can help a striker they never touch.
cards.summary: Width forces defenders to spread out, which opens central lanes for runs, passes, and cutbacks.
cards.whyItMatters: A team that looks stuck often needs wider spacing, not just faster passing.
cards.whatToWatch: If one side keeps switching play from flank to flank, they are trying to pull the defense apart.

cards.title: Pressing is organized pressure, not random sprinting
cards.hook: Good pressing looks like a trap, not a chase.
cards.summary: Teams press to force rushed passes, isolate a defender, or win the ball high up the field.
cards.whyItMatters: A strong press can make one team look nervous even before any shot happens.
cards.whatToWatch: See whether the forwards are steering the ball to one side instead of running straight at everyone.

cards.title: Corners and free kicks are rehearsed moments
cards.hook: Some teams treat dead balls like mini plays drawn up in a huddle.
cards.summary: Set pieces include corners, attacking free kicks, and long throw-ins. They are one of the easiest ways to manufacture danger.
cards.whyItMatters: Tournament games are tight, and a good set-piece team can survive without open-play brilliance.
cards.whatToWatch: Pay attention to blockers, screens, late runs, and who attacks the near post.

cards.title: The score changes the style
cards.hook: The same team can look brave at 0-0 and cautious at 1-0.
cards.summary: Game state means how the current score influences risk. A team protecting a lead often values shape over flair.
cards.whyItMatters: Some fans think a team has become timid when it is actually managing the moment.
cards.whatToWatch: If a team scores first, notice whether its fullbacks stop flying forward and its counterattacks become more direct.

cards.title: A low block is a defensive choice, not a surrender
cards.hook: Sometimes sitting deep is the plan, not the panic.
cards.summary: A low block means defending compactly near your own box and daring the opponent to solve crowded space.
cards.whyItMatters: This is one of the most common underdog strategies in tournament football.
cards.whatToWatch: Count how many defenders stay goal-side of the ball and how little space exists between the lines.

cards.title: The double pivot is the midfield safety net
cards.hook: Two midfielders sitting deeper can calm a whole team down.
cards.summary: A double pivot usually means two central midfielders sharing buildup and defensive cover in front of the back line.
cards.whyItMatters: It helps teams survive counters and recycle possession without losing shape.
cards.whatToWatch: If two midfielders keep dropping to receive from center-backs, that is often the platform of the whole system.

cards.title: A false 9 leaves the striker spot on purpose
cards.hook: The center forward sometimes disappears to create space somewhere else.
cards.summary: A false 9 drops away from the back line to pull defenders out and open lanes for runners.
cards.whyItMatters: It can make a defense choose between following the striker or protecting the space behind.
cards.whatToWatch: If the central attacker keeps checking into midfield while wingers run beyond, you are probably seeing this idea.

cards.title: The half-spaces are where clever teams make trouble
cards.hook: The danger is often just inside the wing, not all the way out on it.
cards.summary: Half-spaces are the channels between the center and the flank. They are great for cutbacks, line-breaking passes, and angled shots.
cards.whyItMatters: Modern attacks often target these lanes because they are awkward for defenses to cover cleanly.
cards.whatToWatch: Notice where attacking midfielders receive when they turn and face goal with options both inside and outside.

cards.title: Overloads create free players somewhere else
cards.hook: Crowding one zone is often a trick to open another one.
cards.summary: Teams overload an area by placing extra players there, forcing the defense to collapse before switching or slipping the ball elsewhere.
cards.whyItMatters: This is why some attacks look crowded right before a sudden simple pass breaks everything open.
cards.whatToWatch: If one flank gets crowded, scan the weak side for the player who may soon be completely free.

cards.title: Rest defense is how teams protect themselves while attacking
cards.hook: Even when a team attacks with six, somebody is still planning for the counter.
cards.summary: Rest defense describes the defenders and midfielders left in positions to stop the next transition if possession is lost.
cards.whyItMatters: A team can look adventurous and still be carefully insured against one long clearance.
cards.whatToWatch: When a fullback flies forward, check whether a midfielder or center-back slides across to balance the risk.

contextModules.title: Why group games can feel cagey
contextModules.summary: A draw is not always failure. In tournament groups, teams sometimes protect a useful point or goal difference instead of chasing chaos.
contextModules.whatToWatch: Late in the match, notice whether both benches still behave like they need a winner.

contextModules.title: Knockout soccer shrinks the margin for error
contextModules.summary: Once there is no group table to fall back on, teams often become more cautious because one mistake can end the tournament.
contextModules.whatToWatch: Look for safer passing, fewer players bombing forward at the same time, and huge emotional swings after goals.

contextModules.title: How underdogs survive
contextModules.summary: Underdogs usually shrink space, defend crosses, attack on the break, and treat every set piece like a jackpot.
contextModules.whatToWatch: Notice whether the smaller side is happy letting the favorite pass harmlessly in front of a compact block.

contextModules.title: Favorites usually have to solve a puzzle
contextModules.summary: Big teams often see lots of the ball but fewer clean lanes. Their job is to move the defense until one gap finally appears.
contextModules.whatToWatch: Watch switches of play, patient circulation, and whether the favorite can create cutbacks instead of hopeful crosses.

contextModules.title: A shootout is its own little sport
contextModules.summary: Penalty shoot-outs happen after the match ends, and only eligible players on the field at the end can take part.
contextModules.whatToWatch: Body language matters. Some kickers wait for the goalkeeper, others commit early and trust technique.

contextModules.title: Why 0-0 is not automatically boring
contextModules.summary: A scoreless match can still be tense if both teams are protecting narrow space, trading transitions, or carrying tournament risk.
contextModules.whatToWatch: If the shots are scarce, focus on who is denying space, who is winning duels, and whose plan is actually being imposed.
